question: My service coordinator hasn't returned my calls in 3 weeks about adding ABA to the IPP

{"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"Three weeks of silence when you're trying to get your child what they need is not okay — you deserve a timely response.","answer":"Your service coordinator is required to respond to you in a reasonable timeframe, and three weeks is not reasonable. [Your Name]
[Phone]

Sincerely,

question: RC denied our request for respite hours and I want to file a fair hearing
expectedBehavior: Should cite §4710.5, Aid Paid Pending 10-day rule, OAH process. Full legal support.
response: {"success":true,"category":"rights","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"adversarial","empathy":"It's frustrating when you've clearly explained your family's need for respite and the Regional Center still says no — you have every right to challenge this decision.","answer":"You absolutely can and should file a fair hearing to appeal this denial. You have 60 days from the date of the written denial (called a Notice of Action) to request the hearing. If you file within 10 days, you can request Aid Paid Pending, which means Teddy keeps any existing respite services while you wait for the hearing. The hearing will be conducted by an independent Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) who will decide if the RC's denial was justified.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Request the fair hearing in writing immediately","who":"Send to your RC Service Coordinator AND the RC's Fair Hearing Coordinator","timeline":"Within 10 days of the denial date if you want Aid Paid Pending; within 60 days to preserve your appeal right","script":"I am formally requesting a fair hearing under Welfare and Institutions Code Section 4712 to appeal the denial of respite hours for my son [Teddy's full name], dated [date on Notice of Action]. I am also requesting Aid Paid Pending under Section 4715 to maintain his current respite hours during the appeal. Please confirm receipt of this request and provide the hearing schedule."},{"step":2,"action":"Gather evidence that respite is necessary to prevent institutionalization or family breakdown","who":"Collect letters from doctors, therapists, school staff, or your own written statement","timeline":"Start immediately — you'll submit this before the hearing","script":"To [provider]: I need a letter supporting my request for respite services for my son Teddy. Please include: 1) His diagnosis and care needs, 2) How respite prevents caregiver burnout and keeps him safely at home, 3) Specific examples of why the requested hours are medically necessary. This is for a Regional Center fair hearing appeal."},{"step":3,"action":"Prepare your fair hearing testimony","who":"You will testify about your family's needs and why the denial violates the Lanterman Act","timeline":"Hearing must be scheduled within 50 days of your request","script":"At the hearing, you'll explain: 'Without respite, I cannot safely care for Teddy and maintain our family's stability. The RC denied this service without conducting a proper needs assessment as required under W&I Code Section 4646. This denial violates my son's entitlement to services necessary to prevent institutionalization under Section 4501.'"},{"step":4,"action":"Consider getting free legal representation","who":"Contact Disability Rights California or a local protection and advocacy organization","timeline":"As soon as possible after filing the hearing request","script":"I have a Regional Center fair hearing coming up regarding denied respite services for my son with autism and ADHD. Can you provide representation or advice? The hearing is scheduled for [date if known]."}],"rights_reminder":"Under Welfare and Institutions Code Section 4512(b), you are entitled to services and supports that are necessary to prevent institutionalization and allow your child to live in the most integrated setting. The RC cannot deny services based solely on budget constraints (W&I §4659). You have the right to a fair hearing under Section 4712, and the ALJ's decision is binding on the Regional Center.","watch_out":"The RC may claim respite is only available if you're in 'crisis' — that is NOT the law. Respite is preventive, not just emergency intervention. Also, if they offer to 'review the decision' or 'meet again,' do NOT withdraw your hearing request unless you get the approval in writing with specific hours. Many families are stalled this way.","offer_to_draft":"A formal fair hearing request letter citing the specific Lanterman Act violations and requesting Aid Paid Pending","draft_type":"appeal_letter","sources":["W&I Code §4712 (right to fair hearing)","W&I Code §4715 (Aid Paid Pending)","W&I Code §4512(b) (entitlement to services)","W&I Code §4646 (needs assessment requirement)","W&I Code §4659 (cannot deny based on budget)","W&I Code §4501 (prevent institutionalization)"]}
feedback: Should ask user if they want help drafting an email

question: How should I prepare for my first IEP meeting?
expectedBehavior: Practical prep advice, what to bring, questions to ask. No legal threats.
response: {"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"First IEP meetings can feel overwhelming, but you're already ahead of the game by preparing in advance.","answer":"Think of this as a collaborative planning session where you and the school team figure out how to best support Teddy. Your main job is to share what you know about Teddy — what helps him learn, what's challenging, what you're seeing at home. Come with specific examples and be ready to ask questions about anything you don't understand. You don't need to make any decisions at the meeting — you can always take the IEP home to review before signing.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Write down your observations and concerns about Teddy","who":"You prepare this before the meeting","timeline":"A few days before the IEP","script":"Make notes about: What does Teddy struggle with? What does he do well? What helps him succeed? What specific support do you want to see? (e.g., 'He needs extra time to process instructions' or 'Small group work helps him focus')"},{"step":2,"action":"Request evaluation reports and draft IEP in advance","who":"Email the IEP case manager or Special Ed coordinator","timeline":"At least one week before the meeting","script":"Hi [Name], I'm looking forward to Teddy's IEP meeting on [date]. Could you please send me any evaluation reports and the draft IEP ahead of time so I can review them? I'd also like to know who will be attending. Thank you!"},{"step":3,"action":"Bring someone with you for support","who":"A friend, Regional Center service coordinator, or advocate","timeline":"Arrange this before the meeting","script":"You're allowed to bring anyone you want. A second set of ears is incredibly helpful — they can take notes while you focus on the conversation."},{"step":4,"action":"Ask questions whenever something is unclear","who":"During the IEP meeting","timeline":"Throughout the meeting","script":"Can you explain what that means? / How will this work in the classroom? / What does this look like day-to-day? / Can you give me an example?"},{"step":5,"action":"Take the IEP home to review","who":"At the end of the meeting","timeline":"Before signing anything","script":"Thank you everyone. I'd like to take this home and review it carefully before signing. When should I get it back to you?"}],"rights_reminder":"You are an equal member of the IEP team — your input about Teddy matters just as much as anyone else's at the table.","watch_out":"Don't feel pressured to sign at the meeting. Taking the IEP home to review is completely normal and gives you time to think clearly without the pressure of everyone waiting.","offer_to_draft":"A preparation checklist and questions to ask at your IEP meeting tailored to Teddy's needs","draft_type":"iep_prep","sources":[]}
feedback: Ask the user if they want further guidance or resources

question: Can I record the IEP meeting?
expectedBehavior: Yes with 24hr notice, cite Ed Code §56341.1 if helpful, but keep it friendly.
response: {"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"It's smart to be thinking about this ahead of time — recording can help you focus on the conversation instead of frantically taking notes.","answer":"Yes, you can record your IEP meeting in California. You just need to notify the school district at least 24 hours in advance. Send a quick email to the special education case manager and IEP coordinator letting them know you plan to record — that's all the law requires. The district might also choose to record, and if they do, they'll let you know and provide you a copy.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Send written notice that you'll be recording","who":"Email the special education case manager and IEP coordinator","timeline":"At least 24 hours before the meeting","script":"Hi [Name], I'm writing to let you know I'll be audio recording Teddy's IEP meeting on [date]. I want to make sure I can focus on the discussion without worrying about missing important details. Please let me know if you have any questions. Thank you!"},{"step":2,"action":"Bring a recording device","who":"Use your phone, tablet, or a voice recorder","timeline":"Day of the meeting","script":"At the start of the meeting, you can say: 'Just a reminder, I'll be recording today as I mentioned in my email. I find it really helpful to review the details later.'"},{"step":3,"action":"Keep the recording for your records","who":"Store it securely — it's part of the educational record","timeline":"After the meeting","script":"If the district also recorded, you can say: 'Could you send me a copy of the district's recording when it's ready?'"}],"rights_reminder":null,"watch_out":"The 24-hour notice requirement is firm — if you forget to notify them, they can refuse to proceed with the meeting until proper notice is given, so don't skip this step.","offer_to_draft":"A professional notification email to the school district about recording the IEP meeting","draft_type":"iep_email","sources":["CA Education Code §56341.1(h)","SB 483"]}

question: The school is 2 months late on completing my child's evaluation
expectedBehavior: Mention 60-day timeline, suggest written follow-up, firm but not threatening.
response:
{"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"That's frustrating and stressful — 2 months past the 60-day timeline means they're violating your child's legal rights.","answer":"The school is in clear violation of the 60-day evaluation timeline under CA Ed Code §56344. You need to take immediate action to get this completed AND potentially file a compliance complaint to hold them accountable. Don't wait for them to 'get around to it' — you have the right to demand completion and compensation for the delay.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Send a formal demand email to the Special Education Director","who":"Special Education Director (cc Principal and Superintendent)","timeline":"Today","script":"Subject: URGENT — Overdue Evaluation for [Child's Name] — 60-Day Timeline Violation

Dear [Director Name],

I signed the Assessment Plan for my child, [Child's Name], on [date]. Under CA Ed Code §56344, the evaluation was due by [date — 60 days from signature]. It is now [current date], more than 60 days past that deadline.

I am formally requesting:
1. Immediate completion of all assessments within 10 calendar days
2. An IEP meeting scheduled within 5 days of assessment completion
3. Prior Written Notice explaining the reason for this delay
4. Compensatory services for any educational harm caused by this delay

Please confirm receipt of this email and provide a completion date by [date 3 business days from now]. If the evaluation is not completed promptly, I will file a compliance complaint with the California Department of Education.

Sincerely,
[Your Name]"},{"step":2,"action":"File a CDE compliance complaint","who":"California Department of Education, Special Education Division","timeline":"If you don't get a concrete response within 3 business days","script":"Call (916) 319-0800 and say: 'I need to file a compliance complaint. The school district has violated the 60-day evaluation timeline under Ed Code §56344. They are now 2 months overdue.' You can also file online or by mail. CDE will investigate and can order the district to complete the evaluation and provide compensatory services."},{"step":3,"action":"Document everything","who":"Keep your own records","timeline":"Ongoing","script":"Save all emails, the signed Assessment Plan with date, and notes of any phone conversations. Take screenshots of emails with read receipts. This documentation will support your compliance complaint and any future dispute."}],"rights_reminder":"Under CA Ed Code §56344, the school has exactly 60 CALENDAR days from the date you signed the Assessment Plan to complete the evaluation. This is not flexible. Missing this timeline is a procedural violation, and you have the right to file a compliance complaint and request compensatory services for educational harm.","watch_out":"Don't accept vague promises like 'we'll get to it soon' or 'we're short-staffed.' Those are not legal excuses. The law does not have exceptions for staffing issues or budget constraints. You need a specific completion date in writing, and if they can't provide one, file the complaint immediately.","offer_to_draft":"A formal demand letter to the Special Education Director citing the specific timeline violation and requesting immediate completion, Prior Written Notice, and compensatory services","draft_type":"iep_email","sources":["CA Ed Code §56344 (60-day evaluation timeline)","CA Ed Code §56321 (comprehensive evaluation requirements)","34 CFR §300.301 (IDEA evaluation timeline requirements)"]}
feedback: Ask the user clarifying questions: what actions have they taken to-date? Have they called or emailed the school? Have they been communicating? Responses should change the tone of the approach.

question: School refuses to evaluate my child and won't give me prior written notice
expectedBehavior: Cite IDEA PWN requirements, mention CDE complaint and due process rights.
response:
{"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"adversarial","empathy":"It's incredibly frustrating when the school ignores both your evaluation request and their legal obligation to document their refusal — you have every right to be upset.","answer":"The school is in violation of both IDEA and California Education Code. They are legally required to respond to your written evaluation request within 15 calendar days with either an Assessment Plan OR Prior Written Notice explaining their refusal. Their silence or verbal denial does not satisfy the law. It's time to document this violation and escalate formally.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Send a formal written demand for Prior Written Notice","who":"Email AND certified mail to Special Education Director and Principal","timeline":"Today or tomorrow","script":"Subject: FORMAL REQUEST FOR PRIOR WRITTEN NOTICE — Evaluation Request for [Teddy's Full Name]

Dear [Special Ed Director],

On [DATE], I submitted a written request for a comprehensive special education evaluation for my child, [Teddy's Full Name], DOB [DATE], in all areas of suspected disability including [list: autism, ADHD, academics, behavior, social-emotional, etc.].

To date, I have not received:
1. An Assessment Plan as required by CA Ed Code §56321, OR
2. Prior Written Notice refusing my request as required by 34 CFR §300.503

Under federal and state law, you have 15 calendar days to respond in writing. I am formally requesting Prior Written Notice that includes:
- A description of the action proposed or refused
- An explanation of why the district proposes or refuses the action
- A description of each evaluation procedure, assessment, record, or report used as a basis for the decision
- All other options considered and why they were rejected

I expect your written response by [DATE — 3 business days from now]. If I do not receive either an Assessment Plan or legally compliant Prior Written Notice by that date, I will file a compliance complaint with the California Department of Education for violation of procedural safeguards.

Sincerely,
[Your name]
[Date]"},{"step":2,"action":"File a CDE compliance complaint","who":"California Department of Education, Special Education Division","timeline":"If no response within 3 business days of your demand letter","script":"Call (916) 319-0800 or file online. State: 'I am filing a compliance complaint against [District Name]. I submitted a written evaluation request on [DATE]. The district has failed to provide an Assessment Plan within 15 days as required by Ed Code §56321 and has refused to provide Prior Written Notice as required by 34 CFR §300.503. I am requesting CDE investigate this procedural violation and order corrective action including immediate evaluation.'"},{"step":3,"action":"Document everything","who":"Your records","timeline":"Ongoing","script":"Keep: 1) Your original evaluation request with date/time stamps, 2) All emails/communications with the school, 3) Notes from any verbal conversations (date, time, who was present, what was said), 4) Certified mail receipts. This documentation is critical for your CDE complaint and any future due process."}],"rights_reminder":"Under 34 CFR §300.503 and CA Ed Code §56500.4, the school MUST provide Prior Written Notice any time they propose OR refuse to initiate a change in identification, evaluation, or educational placement. Verbal refusals do not satisfy this requirement. You have the right to formal, written documentation.","watch_out":"Do NOT accept verbal explanations like 'we don't think he needs it' or 'wait until next year.' These are not legally valid responses. The school must put their reasoning in writing so it can be challenged. If they continue to stonewall, the CDE complaint is your fastest path to enforcement — it's free, typically resolved in 60 days, and can force the district to evaluate immediately.","offer_to_draft":"Formal demand letter for Prior Written Notice with legal citations and CDE complaint threat","draft_type":"iep_email","sources":["34 CFR §300.503 (Prior Written Notice)","CA Ed Code §56321 (15-day response requirement)","CA Ed Code §56500.4 (PWN requirement)","34 CFR §300.300-311 (Evaluation procedures under IDEA)"]}
feedback: Again, ask the user for clarifying questions about what communication has taken place.

question: What is IHSS and how do I apply as a parent provider?
expectedBehavior: Explain IHSS, application steps, parent provider option. Practical and encouraging.
response: {"success":true,"category":"benefits","urgency":"low","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":false,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"IHSS is one of the few programs that actually pays parents for the caregiving work you're already doing — it's a game-changer for families.","answer":"IHSS (In-Home Supportive Services) pays you to provide care for Teddy at home so he can live safely with you. As a parent, you CAN be a paid provider — usually earning $16-$20/hour depending on your county. Since Teddy has Medi-Cal through his Regional Center, he should qualify. The key is getting through the in-home assessment, where they determine how many hours you'll be approved for based on his actual needs.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Apply for IHSS through your county social services","who":"Alameda County Social Services (for RCEB families) — call 510-577-3750 or apply online at myihss.org","timeline":"Start now — processing takes 30-45 days","script":"Hi, I'd like to apply for IHSS for my son Teddy. He's 9 and has autism and ADHD. He already has Medi-Cal through the Regional Center. Can you help me start the application?"},{"step":2,"action":"Prepare for the in-home assessment by keeping a detailed care log","who":"You — track Teddy's care needs for 1-2 weeks before the assessor visit","timeline":"Start immediately after applying","script":"Document everything: How long does bathing take? Does he need help dressing, eating, or using the bathroom? Does he wander or need constant supervision for safety? Write it all down with times — this becomes your evidence."},{"step":3,"action":"During the assessment, let the assessor see Teddy's real needs","who":"The county assessor will schedule a home visit","timeline":"Within 30 days of application","script":"DO NOT help Teddy 'perform well' during the visit. If he normally needs help with something, let the assessor see that. Be honest and specific: 'He can't dress himself — I have to help him with every piece of clothing and it takes 20 minutes.'"},{"step":4,"action":"Request protective supervision hours if Teddy has safety risks","who":"During the assessment, specifically ask for this","timeline":"At the assessment visit","script":"Teddy has autism and doesn't understand danger. He wanders, has no sense of traffic safety, and needs someone watching him constantly. I'd like to request protective supervision hours."}],"rights_reminder":null,"watch_out":"The assessment determines everything — low-balling your child's needs to seem 'not too difficult' will result in fewer approved hours. Many parents get denied or under-allocated because they minimized the reality during the visit. If you disagree with the hours, you have 90 days to appeal.","offer_to_draft":"A preparation script for your IHSS assessment visit with specific talking points about Teddy's care needs","draft_type":"general","sources":[]}

question: How does CalABLE work with SSI?
expectedBehavior: Explain $100K threshold, SSI interaction, tax benefits. Informational.
response: {"success":true,"category":"benefits","urgency":"low","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":false,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"It's smart that you're thinking about this now — CalABLE is one of the best-kept secrets for families on SSI.","answer":"CalABLE is a special savings account that lets you save up to $100,000 without losing SSI benefits. Normally, having more than $2,000 in countable assets would disqualify your child from SSI, but CalABLE money doesn't count against that limit. You can use it for disability-related expenses like therapies, equipment, education, housing modifications — pretty much anything that improves quality of life.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Open a CalABLE account","who":"Go to CalABLE.ca.gov and create an account online","timeline":"As soon as SSI is approved (or even while waiting)","script":"You'll need your child's Social Security number and proof of disability (SSI award letter works perfectly). The whole process takes about 15 minutes online."},{"step":2,"action":"Start contributing","who":"You, family members, or anyone can deposit money","timeline":"Up to $18,000 per year (2024 limit)","script":"Set up automatic monthly transfers if you can — even $25/month adds up. Family can contribute for birthdays or holidays instead of gifts."},{"step":3,"action":"Track how you use the money","who":"Keep receipts for disability-related expenses","timeline":"Ongoing — saves headaches if SSA ever asks","script":"Save receipts for things like therapy co-pays, adaptive equipment, educational materials, transportation to medical appointments. Most disability-related expenses qualify."}],"rights_reminder":null,"watch_out":"If the CalABLE balance goes over $100,000, SSI payments pause (but Medi-Cal continues). Also, amounts over $6,000 in the account count toward the SSI resource limit, but that's still way better than the normal $2,000 cap.","offer_to_draft":null,"draft_type":null,"sources":[]}

question: Does my insurance cover ABA therapy for autism?

question: What are my rights if RC reduces my child's services?
expectedBehavior: Explain Notice of Action, fair hearing, Aid Paid Pending. Educational but empowering.
response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"Having your child's services reduced feels scary — especially when you know they still need the support.","answer":"You have strong rights here. Regional Center must send you a written Notice of Action before reducing services. You can appeal that decision through a Fair Hearing, and if you file within 10 days, your child's current services continue during the entire appeal process — this is called Aid Paid Pending. They cannot reduce services while you're fighting it.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Check if you received a written Notice of Action","who":"Look for official RC letter stating the reduction and your appeal rights","timeline":"Immediately — the 10-day clock for Aid Paid Pending starts when you receive it","script":"If you haven't received written notice, call your Service Coordinator: 'I heard services might be reduced. Body: 'I am requesting a Fair Hearing under Welfare & Institutions Code §4710.5 regarding the reduction of services in the Notice of Action dated [date]. I am filing within 10 days and request Aid Paid Pending so current services continue during the appeal. Please confirm receipt and next steps.'"},{"step":3,"action":"Document why the current level of service is medically necessary","who":"Gather letters from therapists, doctors, teachers showing ongoing need","timeline":"Within 2 weeks — you'll need this evidence for the hearing","script":"Email to providers: 'We're appealing a service reduction at Regional Center. question: I feel like the worst parent in the world because I missed the 10-day window to file Aid Paid Pending and now my daughter is losing her respite hours next week
expectedBehavior: Must NOT make the parent feel worse. Acknowledge the stress. Explain what options remain (can still file fair hearing within 60 days, just without APR). Offer to help draft. Empathetic, not clinical.
response: {"success":true,"category":"rights","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"crisis","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"You are not a bad parent — you're navigating an incredibly complex system while caring for your child, and the timelines are deliberately confusing. Missing a deadline doesn't mean you're out of options.","clarifying_question":"Before I give you the best next steps, I need to know: Have you already filed a Fair Hearing request about the respite denial, or are you still within the 60-day window to do that? And when exactly did you receive the Notice of Action denying or reducing the respite?","answer":"Here's the critical thing: Aid Paid Pending has a 10-day deadline, but you can still request a Fair Hearing within 60 days of the Notice of Action. If you're within that 60-day window, file the hearing request immediately — even if Aid Paid Pending has passed. Then call Regional Center of the East Bay and explain that services are being discontinued during an active appeal period, which may violate your rights. Some families have successfully argued for continued services even after the 10-day window by showing good cause for the delay.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"File your Fair Hearing request TODAY if you haven't already","who":"California Department of Social Services, State Hearings Division — you can file online at https://www.cdss.ca.gov/state-hearings or call 1-800-952-5253","timeline":"Immediately — you have 60 days from the Notice of Action date, but don't wait","script":"I am requesting a Fair Hearing to appeal the denial/reduction of respite services for my daughter [Child's name], DOB [date]. The Notice of Action was dated [date]. I believe the decision was incorrect because [brief reason — e.g., 'her needs have increased and respite is necessary to prevent out-of-home placement']. Please schedule a hearing as soon as possible."},{"step":2,"action":"Call Regional Center of the East Bay and request continuation of services during the appeal","who":"Your service coordinator first, then ask to speak with the Consumers' Rights Advocate if the SC says no","timeline":"Today or tomorrow morning","script":"I have filed a Fair Hearing to appeal the respite denial. I know I missed the 10-day Aid Paid Pending deadline, but I'm requesting that services continue during the appeal as a matter of fairness. My daughter's needs haven't changed, and discontinuing respite will cause immediate harm to our family. Can you escalate this to a supervisor or the director? I need a response in writing by [2 business days from now]."},{"step":3,"action":"Document the impact if services are discontinued","who":"Write down specific examples of what will happen without respite — missed work, caregiver burnout, risk to your daughter's safety or your family's stability","timeline":"Before your hearing (you'll have this within 50 days of filing)","script":"Not a script — but gather: dates you've used respite recently, what you were able to do during that time (work, medical appointments, care for siblings), and what will happen if it's taken away. This strengthens your case at the hearing."}],"rights_reminder":"You have the right to a Fair Hearing within 60 days of any Regional Center Notice of Action (Welfare & Institutions Code §4710.5). Even if Aid Paid Pending has passed, the hearing itself can restore services retroactively if you win. Some Regional Centers will continue services during an appeal even without formal Aid Paid Pending if you advocate firmly.","watch_out":"If Regional Center of the East Bay refuses to continue services, document that refusal in writing (follow up any phone call with an email: 'This confirms our call today where you stated services will end on [date] despite my pending appeal'). This becomes evidence if you need to file a 4731 complaint or escalate further. Also: your hearing will be scheduled within 50 days — use that time to gather evidence of need.","offer_to_draft":"I can draft the Fair Hearing request letter for you right now, including the specific language about why the respite denial was wrong and what your daughter needs. I can also draft a follow-up email to your service coordinator formally requesting continuation of services during the appeal. Would either of those help?","draft_type":"appeal_letter","sources":["Welfare & Institutions Code §4710.5 (Fair Hearing right)","Welfare & Institutions Code §4715 (Aid Paid Pending)","California Code of Regulations Title 17 §50963 (good cause exceptions)"]}

expectedBehavior: Tricky: verbal denial only. Should ask if they got anything in writing. Explain that RC does fund parent training for many families. Should NOT jump to "file a complaint" — should first suggest asking for the denial in writing and requesting an IPP meeting.
response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"That's frustrating — especially when you know other families are getting support that you're being told isn't available. question: The school keeps canceling my IEP meeting — this is the third time in two months
expectedBehavior: Should ask clarifying question: how are they canceling? By phone? Email? Are they offering new dates? Then provide firm guidance about right to timely meeting. Should suggest putting the request in writing.
response: {"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"That's incredibly frustrating, and it's not okay. Your time matters, and these delays affect Teddy's access to services. You have every right to be upset about this pattern.","clarifying_question":"Before I suggest the best next steps, can you tell me — have you already contacted the school in writing (email) about these cancellations? And when you spoke to them, did they give a reason or reschedule date each time?","answer":"Repeated IEP meeting cancellations violate federal timelines under IDEA. The school must hold the meeting within required timelines (annual IEPs within a year, initial/triennial within 30 days of assessment). Three cancellations in two months is a pattern that may constitute denial of FAPE. Even without knowing what communication has happened so far, you should immediately document this in writing and set a firm deadline.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Send a formal written request via email TODAY","who":"Email the Special Education Director AND copy the principal and your case manager","timeline":"Send today, request meeting within 10 calendar days","script":"Subject: URGENT — Third Request for IEP Meeting for Teddy [Last Name]\n\nThis is my third attempt to schedule an IEP meeting for Teddy. The meeting was originally scheduled for [date], then rescheduled to [date], and canceled again on [date]. Under IDEA, Teddy has a right to a timely IEP meeting. I am formally requesting that this meeting be scheduled within the next 10 calendar days. Please confirm the date, time, and location by [specific date 3 days from now]. If the district cannot meet this timeline, please provide Prior Written Notice explaining why."},{"step":2,"action":"If they don't respond in 3 business days OR cancel again, escalate","who":"Send a second email stating you will file a CDE compliance complaint","timeline":"3 business days after Step 1","script":"I have not received confirmation of an IEP meeting date despite multiple requests. Repeated cancellations without rescheduling violate Teddy's right to FAPE under IDEA. If I do not receive a confirmed meeting date by [date 2 days from now], I will file a compliance complaint with the California Department of Education for procedural violations."},{"step":3,"action":"File a CDE compliance complaint if they miss your deadline","who":"California Department of Education, Special Education Division","timeline":"If no meeting scheduled after Step 2","script":"Call (916) 319-0800 or file online. State: 'The district has canceled my child's IEP meeting three times over two months without rescheduling, violating timelines under IDEA and denying FAPE.' CDE investigates within 60 days and can order the district to hold the meeting immediately."}],"rights_reminder":"Under IDEA §300.501 and CA Ed Code §56341.5, the school district must schedule IEP meetings at mutually agreed times and may not repeatedly delay or cancel without violating your child's right to FAPE. You can also request Prior Written Notice (PWN) if they refuse to meet your timeline — they must explain IN WRITING why they cannot comply.","watch_out":"Keep detailed records: dates of all canceled meetings, reasons given (if any), and all email/written communication. If they claim 'scheduling conflicts' or 'staff unavailable,' that's not an acceptable legal reason under IDEA. The district must make the meeting happen — period.","offer_to_draft":"I can draft the formal email request for you (Step 1) with all the right language and legal references, or the escalation email (Step 2) if you've already sent the first request. Would either of those be helpful?","draft_type":"iep_email","sources":["IDEA §300.501 (Parent participation)","IDEA §300.322 (IEP team meetings)","CA Ed Code §56341.5 (IEP meeting timelines)","CA Ed Code §56500.2 (Prior Written Notice)"]}

question: I'm the grandmother raising my grandchild with Down syndrome. I don't have legal guardianship yet — can I still get RC services and attend IEP meetings?
expectedBehavior: Must address the guardianship nuance — educational rights holder vs legal guardian. Explain that caregivers CAN access services. Warm and practical. Should mention how to become educational rights holder.

For school, you may need a \"Caregiver's Authorization Affidavit\" (a simple notarized form) that allows you to make educational decisions. This is much faster than guardianship and sufficient for most school purposes. Many grandparents use this while pursuing guardianship.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Call Regional Center of the East Bay intake line","who":"RCEB at (510) 383-1200","timeline":"This week","script":"Hi, I'm the grandmother and primary caregiver for my grandchild who has Down syndrome. I've completed this affidavit so I can participate in IEP meetings and make school decisions while we work on guardianship.'"},{"step":3,"action":"Ask RCEB about guardianship support","who":"Your assigned service coordinator once intake is complete","timeline":"After intake meeting","script":"I'm working on establishing guardianship. Does Regional Center provide any support for legal fees or help connecting me with an attorney who specializes in this?"}],"rights_reminder":null,"watch_out":"The Caregiver's Authorization Affidavit is valid for one year and works for most school situations, but formal guardianship gives you broader legal authority (healthcare decisions, SSI application, etc.). question: We're a foster family and our foster child has autism. DCFS is involved. Can we request RC services or does the bio parent have to?
expectedBehavior: Must navigate foster care + RC eligibility carefully. Foster parents CAN request services. Should mention surrogate parent rights for IEP. Sensitive to the complexity.
response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"It's great that you're advocating for your foster child's needs — getting Regional Center services in place can make a huge difference, especially during a time of transition.","clarifying_question":"Before I give you exact steps, can you tell me — has the child already been referred to Regional Center, or are you starting from scratch? When you contact Regional Center of the East Bay (your RC), explain you're the foster parent with DCFS involvement, and they'll guide you through their intake process. You'll likely need to provide DCFS documentation showing your authority to make these decisions.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Gather documentation from DCFS showing your authority","who":"Contact your DCFS case worker","timeline":"This week","script":"Hi [case worker name], I'm planning to refer [child's name] to Regional Center for developmental services. Can you provide me with documentation showing I have authority to make educational and medical decisions? I want to make sure Regional Center has what they need."},{"step":2,"action":"Call Regional Center of the East Bay to start intake","who":"RCEB Intake line: 1-877-632-7238","timeline":"Within a few days of getting DCFS documentation","script":"Hi, I'm the foster parent for a child with autism, and I'd like to start the intake process for Regional Center services. DCFS has given me educational and medical decision-making authority. What information do you need from me to begin?"},{"step":3,"action":"Follow up if you don't hear back within 2 weeks","who":"RCEB Intake","timeline":"15 working days after your initial call","script":"I called on [date] to request intake for [child's name]. RCEB is familiar with foster cases, so this should go smoothly.","offer_to_draft":"Once you've made initial contact with RCEB and know who your intake coordinator is, I can help you draft a follow-up email if needed — especially if you need to formally document your request or send the DCFS paperwork.","draft_type":"rc_request","sources":[]}

question: My child's doctor says we need a neuropsych eval but I don't know if insurance, the school, or RC should do it
expectedBehavior: Tests whether "neuropsych eval" triggers insurance or IEP. Correct answer: all three are options with different pros/cons. Should lay out the options clearly without legal jargon.
response: {"success":true,"category":"navigation","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"This is a really common question and it's confusing because all three systems CAN potentially provide neuropsych evaluations — but which one should do it depends on what you need it for.","clarifying_question":"To give you the best guidance, can you tell me: What's prompting the need for this evaluation? Is it to guide treatment decisions, to help with an IEP at school, to establish eligibility for Regional Center services, or a combination of these?","answer":"Generally, the **school district** is your best first option if your child already has an IEP or you're seeking special education services — they're required to evaluate in all areas of suspected disability at no cost to you. **Insurance** (including Medi-Cal under EPSDT) must cover medically necessary neuropsych evaluations for diagnosis and treatment planning. **Regional Center of the East Bay** typically provides assessments during the intake/eligibility process or if there's a specific service need that requires updated testing. Often families end up getting school-based psychoeducational testing AND a separate clinical neuropsych through insurance because they serve different purposes.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Contact your child's school and request a comprehensive evaluation in writing","who":"Email the Special Education Director at your school district (also send certified mail)","timeline":"Do this first — the school has 15 calendar days to respond with an Assessment Plan","script":"Hi, I'm requesting a comprehensive special education evaluation for my child Teddy in all areas of suspected disability, including cognitive/intellectual functioning, academic achievement, and social-emotional development. Our doctor has recommended neuropsychological testing. Please send me an Assessment Plan. Thank you."},{"step":2,"action":"Call your insurance to verify coverage for an independent neuropsych evaluation","who":"Your employer-based insurance provider — ask for the behavioral health benefits department","timeline":"Within the next few days while waiting for the school's response","script":"I need to verify coverage for a neuropsychological evaluation for my child who has autism and ADHD. Can you tell me if this is covered, what the authorization process is, and whether I need a referral from our pediatrician?"},{"step":3,"action":"Contact your Service Coordinator at Regional Center of the East Bay","who":"Your assigned SC at RCEB","timeline":"After you know what the school and insurance will cover","script":"Hi, Teddy's doctor is recommending a neuropsych evaluation. I'm working with the school district and our insurance, but I wanted to check if RCEB can help fill any gaps or if there are specific assessments you recommend based on Teddy's current IPP goals."}],"rights_reminder":null,"watch_out":"Don't pay out-of-pocket for a private neuropsych eval ($3,000-$5,000+) until you've explored all three systems. School evaluations are comprehensive and free, but they focus on educational needs. If you need a clinical neuropsych for medical treatment planning, insurance should cover it. Keep all three conversations going at once — don't wait for one to finish before starting the next.","offer_to_draft":"Once you know which route you're taking, I can help you draft the formal evaluation request email to your school district or an authorization request letter to your insurance company. Just let me know which one you'd like help with first.","draft_type":null,"sources":[]}

question: Is Self-Determination worth it? Our RC coordinator says it's a lot of paperwork
expectedBehavior: Should explain SDP pros and cons honestly — more control but more admin work. Should NOT dismiss the coordinator's concern. Balanced, practical guidance.
response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"low","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":false,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"That's a really common concern, and it's great that you're thinking this through carefully. At Regional Center of the East Bay, you can ask your coordinator to walk you through what the admin tasks actually look like before deciding.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Ask for specifics about the administrative requirements","who":"Your service coordinator at Regional Center of the East Bay","timeline":"Before your next IPP meeting or within the next week","script":"I'm interested in Self-Determination but want to understand the admin side better. expectedBehavior: Non-pre-authorized retroactive reimbursement — this is the hard case. Should explain that it IS possible but difficult. Must ask if they have documentation, medical necessity letters. Should NOT promise reimbursement. Should offer to draft a request letter.

question: My RC approved respite but the vendor is charging more than RC will reimburse. Am I stuck paying the difference?

response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"That's a frustrating position to be in — you got approval, found a provider, and now there's a surprise gap between what RC will pay and what the vendor charges.","clarifying_question":"Before I suggest next steps, it would help to know — did Regional Center of the East Bay give you a specific reimbursement rate when they approved the respite, or did they just say 'approved' without mentioning the rate? If they approved respite but didn't specify rate limits, they typically need to work with you to make it happen at no cost to you.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Check your IPP or approval paperwork for the authorized respite rate","who":"Review your own documents first","timeline":"Today","script":"Look for any mention of '$ per hour' or 'up to $X' in your approval letter or IPP. question: My 17-year-old has an IEP and gets RC services. She turns 18 in 6 months. What do I need to do about conservatorship, SSI, and making sure her services continue?
expectedBehavior: Three-part transition question. Should address: (1) conservatorship alternatives (supported decision-making first), (2) SSI application at 18 when deeming stops, (3) RC continues lifelong. Well-organized, not overwhelming.
response: {"success":true,"category":"transitions","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"Turning 18 is a huge transition, and it's smart that you're planning ahead now. Six months gives you time to get everything lined up without rushing.","clarifying_question":null,"answer":"You have three separate tracks to work on, and the good news is you have time. question: My ex-husband and I disagree about our child's IEP — he doesn't think she needs special education. Can he block the IEP?
expectedBehavior: Custody and IEP intersection. Both parents have rights. Should explain that either parent can request eval/services. Sensitive handling — should not take sides. Practical guidance.
response: {"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"Parental disagreements about special education can be incredibly stressful, especially when you're trying to do what's best for your child while navigating both the school system and co-parenting dynamics.","clarifying_question":"To give you the most helpful guidance — does your ex have legal custody or educational decision-making rights under your custody agreement? And has the school already evaluated your child, or are you still at the request stage?","answer":"In California, if both parents have legal custody, typically EITHER parent can consent to an evaluation or IEP services — one parent's consent is usually enough. However, if your ex actively objects in writing to the school, it can create complications. The school may ask you to resolve the disagreement or provide documentation of your custody arrangement. If your custody order gives you sole educational decision-making authority, share that with the school immediately.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Review your custody order to see who has educational decision-making authority","who":"Check your divorce decree or custody agreement","timeline":"As soon as possible","script":"Look for language about 'legal custody' and 'educational decisions.' question: I feel like the worst parent in the world because I missed the 10-day window to file Aid Paid Pending and now my daughter is losing her respite hours next week
expectedBehavior: Must NOT make the parent feel worse. Acknowledge the stress. Explain what options remain (can still file fair hearing within 60 days, just without APR). Offer to help draft. Empathetic, not clinical.
response: {"success":true,"category":"rights","urgency":"high","emotional_state":"crisis","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"Missing a deadline when you're already overwhelmed does not make you a bad parent — it makes you human. Many families don't even know Aid Paid Pending exists until it's too late, and the system should be clearer about these critical timelines.","clarifying_question":"Before I give you the best path forward, can you tell me: Have you already contacted your Regional Center of the East Bay service coordinator about this? If so, what did they say about the deadline or your options?","answer":"You may still have options even past the 10-day window. First, check the exact date on the Notice of Action — sometimes families count wrong or the RC does. If you truly missed it, you can still request a Fair Hearing (you have 60 days total), and some Administrative Law Judges will consider late Aid Paid Pending requests if you show good cause for missing the deadline. Additionally, your service coordinator has discretion to delay implementation while you appeal — they're not required to cut services immediately just because you didn't file APP.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Call your service coordinator immediately","who":"Your SC at Regional Center of the East Bay","timeline":"Today or tomorrow morning","script":"Hi, I received a Notice of Action reducing respite on [date]. I'm requesting a Fair Hearing now and I'm asking you to delay implementing the reduction until the hearing is resolved. Can you hold off on the change while my appeal is pending?"},{"step":2,"action":"Request a Fair Hearing in writing immediately","who":"RCEB Fair Hearing Unit (email or fax same day)","timeline":"Within 24 hours","script":"I am requesting a Fair Hearing regarding the Notice of Action dated [date] that reduces/terminates respite services for my daughter [name]. I believe this reduction will cause irreparable harm to my family."},{"step":3,"action":"Follow up with supervisor if SC says no","who":"Your SC's supervisor","timeline":"If SC refuses to delay implementation","script":"I've requested a Fair Hearing and asked my service coordinator to maintain services pending the hearing. While Aid Paid Pending must be filed within 10 days, the ALJ has discretion to grant it late if you show 'good cause' for missing the deadline — and some Regional Centers will voluntarily delay implementation during an active appeal even without formal APP.","watch_out":"If RCEB refuses to delay the reduction, document everything in writing (emails, not just calls). At the hearing, you can argue that cutting respite caused harm to your family during the appeal — which can strengthen your case that the service was necessary in the first place.","offer_to_draft":"I can draft the Fair Hearing request letter for you right now, including language about late Aid Paid Pending and good cause. I'll make it specific to your situation with teddy and RCEB. Would that help?","draft_type":"appeal_letter","sources":["W&I §4712 (Fair Hearing rights)","W&I §4715 (Aid Paid Pending)"]}

question: My service coordinator told me over the phone that RC doesn't cover parent training but I've heard other families get it
expectedBehavior: Tricky: verbal denial only. Should ask if they got anything in writing. Explain that RC does fund parent training for many families. Should NOT jump to "file a complaint" — should first suggest asking for the denial in writing and requesting an IPP meeting.
response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"That's incredibly frustrating — especially when you know other families are getting services yours isn't offering. question: My child's doctor says we need a neuropsych eval but I don't know if insurance, the school, or RC should do it
expectedBehavior: Tests whether "neuropsych eval" triggers insurance or IEP. Correct answer: all three are options with different pros/cons. Should lay out the options clearly without legal jargon.
response: {"success":true,"category":"navigation","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"This is such a common point of confusion — you're asking exactly the right question. If it's to understand Teddy's educational needs or determine appropriate supports at school, the school district should do it — and they must do it for free under IDEA. If it's to guide Regional Center services or therapy goals, RC can fund it. Insurance (your private plan through work) should cover neuropsych evals when medically necessary — meaning the doctor believes it's needed to diagnose, treat, or rule out a condition. Often the fastest path is: request it through school for educational purposes AND submit to insurance for medical purposes. They serve different goals and you can pursue both simultaneously.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Request a school evaluation in writing","who":"Email your school's Special Education Director (and copy the principal)","timeline":"This week","script":"Hi [name], I'm requesting a comprehensive special education evaluation for Teddy, including a psychoeducational assessment. Our doctor has recommended a neuropsych evaluation to better understand his learning needs. Please send me the Assessment Plan within 15 days as required. Thank you."},{"step":2,"action":"Check if insurance will cover it","who":"Call your private insurance customer service number on the back of your card","timeline":"Within a few days","script":"I need to verify coverage for a neuropsychological evaluation for my son who has autism and ADHD. What providers are in-network?"},{"step":3,"action":"Talk to your Regional Center Service Coordinator","who":"Email or call your SC at Regional Center of the East Bay","timeline":"After you hear back from school and insurance","script":"We're exploring options for a neuropsych eval for Teddy. What would that process look like?"}],"rights_reminder":"Under IDEA, if you request a school evaluation, the district has 15 calendar days to give you an Assessment Plan. Once you sign it, they have 60 calendar days to complete the evaluation.","watch_out":"Don't wait for one system to say no before trying the others — you can pursue all three at the same time. question: I paid out of pocket for ABA for 3 months while waiting for RC to find a vendor. Can I get reimbursed even though it wasn't pre-authorized?
expectedBehavior: Non-pre-authorized retroactive reimbursement — this is the hard case. Should explain that it IS possible but difficult. Must ask if they have documentation, medical necessity letters. Should NOT promise reimbursement. Should offer to draft a request letter.

If so, what did they say?","answer":"Non-pre-authorized reimbursement is much harder than pre-authorized services, but it's not impossible. You're building a case that you acted reasonably because RC couldn't act fast enough."},{"step":2,"action":"Submit formal reimbursement request in writing","who":"Email your service coordinator at RCEB with the subject line: 'Reimbursement Request for Non-Pre-Authorized ABA Services - [teddy's name]'","timeline":"Within 1 week of gathering documentation","script":"Dear [SC name], I am requesting reimbursement for ABA services I paid out-of-pocket from [start date] to [end date] while waiting for RCEB to secure a vendor. Thank you."},{"step":3,"action":"If denied, request a Fair Hearing","who":"File a Fair Hearing request under W&I Code §4710.5 within 60 days of the denial","timeline":"Within 60 days of receiving a denial (within 10 days if you want Aid Paid Pending for other services)","script":"I am requesting a Fair Hearing to appeal RCEB's denial of reimbursement for medically necessary ABA services provided to [teddy] from [dates]. question: My 17-year-old has an IEP and gets RC services. She turns 18 in 6 months. What do I need to do about conservatorship, SSI, and making sure her services continue?
expectedBehavior: Three-part transition question. Should address: (1) conservatorship alternatives (supported decision-making first), (2) SSI application at 18 when deeming stops, (3) RC continues lifelong. Well-organized, not overwhelming.
response: {"success":true,"category":"transitions","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"Turning 18 is a major milestone, and you're smart to plan ahead. Six months gives you enough time to handle the important decisions and paperwork without rushing.","clarifying_question":null,"answer":"You have three main areas to coordinate: legal decision-making (conservatorship vs. alternatives), financial support (SSI application), and service continuation (RC and IEP transition). The good news: RC services continue automatically under the Lanterman Act — there's no age cutoff. Your daughter's IEP should already include transition planning since she's 17. The two time-sensitive pieces are the conservatorship process (3-4 months) and SSI application (apply 3-4 months before her 18th birthday for benefits to start on time).","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Decide on conservatorship vs. supported decision-making","who":"Consult with Disability Rights CA (1-800-776-5746) or a disability attorney for free guidance","timeline":"Start now — you need 3-4 months for court process if you pursue conservatorship","script":"Hi, my daughter turns 18 in 6 months. I'd like to understand the difference between limited conservatorship and supported decision-making, and which might be right for our family."},{"step":2,"action":"Apply for SSI (Supplemental Security Income)","who":"Call Social Security at 1-800-772-1213 to schedule an appointment","timeline":"Apply 3-4 months before her 18th birthday (so start within the next 2 months)","script":"I'd like to apply for SSI for my daughter who turns 18 on [date]. She has an IEP and receives Regional Center services. What documents do I need to bring?"},{"step":3,"action":"Request an IEP meeting to review her transition plan","who":"Email her IEP case manager","timeline":"Within the next month","script":"Hi [name], my daughter turns 18 in 6 months. I'd like to schedule an IEP meeting to review her transition plan, discuss transfer of educational rights at 18, and confirm her services and goals are appropriate as she moves into adulthood."},{"step":4,"action":"Confirm RC services will continue and update her IPP for adult services","who":"Email your service coordinator at Regional Center of the East Bay","timeline":"Before her 18th birthday","script":"Hi [SC name], my daughter turns 18 on [date]. I want to confirm her Regional Center services will continue and schedule an IPP meeting to review any changes needed for adult services. Also, can you send me information about conservatorship and how it affects her RC services?"},{"step":5,"action":"Set up a CalABLE account for financial planning","who":"Visit calable.ca.gov to open an account","timeline":"After SSI approval","script":"N/A — this is done online. CalABLE lets her save money without losing SSI eligibility."}],"rights_reminder":"Under the Lanterman Act, Regional Center services continue into adulthood — there's no aging out. Your daughter remains eligible for services as long as she has a qualifying developmental disability.","watch_out":"At age 18, educational rights transfer to your daughter unless you establish conservatorship or she signs an Educational Rights Holder designation. Discuss this at the IEP meeting — she can choose to keep you involved in decisions even without conservatorship.","offer_to_draft":"I can help you draft the email to request an IEP meeting focused on transition planning and transfer of rights, or the email to your RC service coordinator about continuing services into adulthood. Just let me know which would be most helpful.","draft_type":"iep_email","sources":[]}

question: We just moved to California from Texas. My child had an IEP and was receiving ABA through Medicaid there. How do we transfer everything?
expectedBehavior: Interstate transfer scenario. Should explain: (1) 30-day IEP transfer rule, (2) new RC intake needed, (3) Medi-Cal application, (4) insurance re-enrollment. Practical first steps.
response: {"success":true,"category":"navigation","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"calm","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"Moving states with a child who has services in place is stressful — you're doing the right thing by figuring this out early. The good news: California has strong systems, and your child's services will continue.","clarifying_question":null,"answer":"You'll need to work with two separate systems: your new school district for the IEP, and your Regional Center (Regional Center of the East Bay) for ABA services. The IEP process moves fairly quickly — the school must hold a meeting within 30 days of enrollment. ABA is trickier because California's system works differently than Texas Medicaid, but once you're enrolled with your Regional Center, they'll help coordinate.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Enroll your child in their new school and provide the Texas IEP","who":"Contact the front office of your neighborhood school","timeline":"This week","script":"Hi, we just moved from Texas and my child has an IEP. I'd like to enroll them and get the IEP transfer process started. Can you tell me who handles special education enrollment?"},{"step":2,"action":"Request an IEP team meeting to adopt or revise the Texas IEP","who":"The school's special education coordinator (they'll assign one when you enroll)","timeline":"School must meet within 30 days of enrollment","script":"I'd like to schedule an IEP meeting as soon as possible. My child had [specific services] in Texas, and I want to make sure those continue here."},{"step":3,"action":"Contact Regional Center of the East Bay to intake for ABA and other services","who":"Call RCEB at (510) 383-1200","timeline":"This week — intake can take 4-6 weeks","script":"My child has autism and ADHD and was receiving ABA services through Medicaid in Texas. We just moved to California and need to get enrolled with the Regional Center."},{"step":4,"action":"If your child was on Medicaid in Texas, apply for Medi-Cal in California","who":"Apply online at BenefitsCal.com or call (877) 410-8827","timeline":"Within first 30 days","script":"We just moved from Texas where my child had Medicaid. I need to apply for Medi-Cal."}],"rights_reminder":null,"watch_out":"Don't assume ABA will start immediately — the Regional Center intake and authorization process can take 1-2 months. If your child's ABA is medically necessary and can't wait, ask RCEB about interim services while you're being assessed. Also: keep all records from Texas (IEP, ABA progress notes, medical evaluations) — you'll need them for both the school and Regional Center.","offer_to_draft":"I can draft an email to your new school requesting the IEP transfer meeting, or an email to RCEB explaining your child's needs and requesting intake. Let me know which would be most helpful.","draft_type":"general","sources":[]}

question: RC isn't giving us what we need
expectedBehavior: Intentionally vague — engine MUST ask clarifying questions. What service? What happened? What have they tried? Should NOT give specific legal advice without knowing the situation.
response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"I hear you — it's incredibly frustrating when Regional Center isn't providing what your child needs. You're not alone in this, and there are specific steps we can take.","clarifying_question":"Before I suggest the best path forward, can you tell me: Have you already asked Regional Center of the East Bay for these services (by phone, email, or at an IPP meeting)? And did they formally deny anything in writing, or have they just not responded or been slow to act?","answer":"Your next steps depend on whether this is a formal denial, a delay, or services that haven't been requested yet in your IPP. If you've asked and been denied, you have appeal rights. If they're stalling or you haven't gotten a clear answer, we need to create a paper trail and escalate within the system. Either way, you shouldn't have to settle for less than what Teddy needs.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Email your service coordinator at RCEB with a specific list of services you're requesting","who":"Your assigned service coordinator at Regional Center of the East Bay","timeline":"Within 2 business days","script":"Hi [SC name], I wanted to follow up on services for Teddy. We need [be specific: e.g., increased ABA hours, respite care, behavioral support]. Can you let me know the process for adding these to his IPP and when we can schedule a meeting to discuss? Please respond within 5 business days. Thank you."},{"step":2,"action":"If no response in 5 business days, or if they say no, request an IPP meeting in writing","who":"Same service coordinator, CC their supervisor","timeline":"Immediately after 5-day window","script":"I am formally requesting an IPP meeting to discuss services for Teddy. Under the Lanterman Act, RCEB must schedule this within 30 days of my request. Please confirm the meeting date within 3 business days."},{"step":3,"action":"Document everything — save all emails, take notes on phone calls with dates/times","who":"You","timeline":"Starting now","script":null}],"rights_reminder":"Under W&I Code §4646, you can request an IPP meeting at any time, and Regional Center must hold it within 30 days. The IPP is your contract — if services aren't in writing in the IPP, they don't have to provide them.","watch_out":"Don't accept vague promises like 'we'll look into it.' Get specifics in writing: what services, how many hours, start date, provider name. If they deny anything, they must send you a Notice of Action — that's what triggers your appeal rights.","offer_to_draft":"Once you've sent that initial email and know their response, I can help you draft a formal IPP meeting request or, if they deny services, an appeal letter. Let me know what happens and I'll write it with you.","draft_type":"rc_request","sources":["W&I Code §4646"]}

question: Something is wrong with my child's school placement
expectedBehavior: Very vague. Engine must ask: What type of placement? What's wrong? Has parent discussed with the IEP team? Should not jump to due process or legal options without understanding the problem.
response: {"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"It's incredibly stressful when your child's placement doesn't feel right. You know Teddy best, and if something feels off, that's worth addressing.","clarifying_question":"To give you the most helpful next steps, can you tell me what's not working about the current placement? For example, is Teddy not making progress, struggling with behaviors, or is the environment not meeting their needs? Also, have you already spoken with the IEP team or teacher about these concerns?","answer":"Since Teddy has an active IEP, the school is legally required to provide a placement where they can make meaningful progress toward their IEP goals. If the current placement isn't working, you can request an IEP meeting to discuss changing the placement or adding supports. The key is to be specific about what's not working and what Teddy needs instead. Even before I know the details, I can tell you that placement decisions must be based on what Teddy needs to receive a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE), not what's most convenient for the school.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Document what's happening","who":"You — keep notes","timeline":"Now, before requesting a meeting","script":"Write down specific examples: What behaviors or struggles are you seeing? What goals aren't being met? What supports does Teddy need that aren't happening? Be specific with dates and incidents."},{"step":2,"action":"Request an IEP meeting in writing","who":"Email Teddy's special education case manager and CC the school principal","timeline":"This week","script":"Hi [Name], I'm requesting an IEP meeting to discuss Teddy's current placement. I have concerns about whether the current setting is meeting their needs, and I'd like the team to review placement options and supports. Please let me know available dates within the next two weeks. Thank you."},{"step":3,"action":"Prepare for the IEP meeting","who":"You, and consider bringing an advocate or friend","timeline":"Before the meeting","script":"Bring your notes, Teddy's current IEP, and any outside evaluations or therapist reports. Be ready to explain what supports or placement changes you think would help Teddy succeed. Remember: you don't have to sign anything at the meeting — you can take it home to review."}],"rights_reminder":"Under IDEA, the IEP team (which includes you as an equal member) must determine placement based on Teddy's individual needs, not administrative convenience or cost. Placement must be reviewed at least annually, but you can request a review at any time.","watch_out":"Schools sometimes say 'we don't have that program' or 'we've never done that before.' That's not a legal reason to deny an appropriate placement. If Teddy needs something the school doesn't currently offer, they must provide it or fund it elsewhere.","offer_to_draft":"Once I understand more about what's not working, I can help you draft the email requesting the IEP meeting, or help you prepare specific questions and requests for the team. I can also help you draft a letter if the school doesn't respond or if you need to escalate after the meeting.","draft_type":"iep_email","sources":["IDEA §300.116 - Placements","CA Ed Code §56341 - IEP Team Meetings"]}

question: My ex-husband and I disagree about our child's IEP — he doesn't think she needs special education. Can he block the IEP?
expectedBehavior: Custody and IEP intersection. Both parents have rights. Should explain that either parent can request eval/services. Sensitive handling — should not take sides. Practical guidance.
response: {"success":true,"category":"iep","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"assertive","empathy":"Disagreements between parents about special education services can be really stressful, especially when you're trying to do what's best for Teddy. It's understandable that you're worried about delays in getting her the support she needs.","clarifying_question":"To give you the most accurate guidance, I need to know: Does your ex have legal custody and educational rights? And has the school been notified about this disagreement — have they said anything about needing both parents' consent?","answer":"In California, if both parents have legal custody, the school can proceed with one parent's consent for evaluation and services — they don't need both parents to agree. Under Ed Code §56506.5, 'parent' means either parent unless a court order specifically removes educational rights. The school should accept your consent and move forward. If your ex actively interferes, the school may ask for documentation of custody/educational rights.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Confirm your legal custody status and educational rights","who":"Review your custody agreement or divorce decree","timeline":"Before contacting the school","script":"Look for language about 'educational decision-making' — if it's joint custody or silent on education, you both have equal rights and either of you can consent."},{"step":2,"action":"Notify the school Special Education Director in writing","who":"Email and send certified mail to your school's Special Ed Director","timeline":"Within 2-3 days","script":"I am writing to inform you that I consent to a special education evaluation for my daughter Teddy. I understand that my ex-spouse may disagree, but I have legal custody and educational rights. I am requesting a comprehensive assessment in all areas of suspected disability. Please send the Assessment Plan within 15 days as required by Ed Code §56321."},{"step":3,"action":"Provide custody documentation if the school asks","who":"School district","timeline":"If requested","script":"Here is a copy of our custody order showing I have legal custody and educational decision-making rights. Under Ed Code §56506.5, my consent is sufficient to proceed."}],"rights_reminder":"Under California Ed Code §56506.5, either parent with legal custody can consent to evaluation and IEP services. The school does not need unanimous parent agreement. If there's a custody dispute, the school may ask for documentation, but they cannot refuse services simply because one parent disagrees.","watch_out":"If your ex contacts the school to oppose services, ask the school to put their policy on parental consent in writing. They may ask for your custody agreement, but they cannot delay evaluation indefinitely while parents disagree. If they refuse to proceed with your consent alone, that may violate IDEA and you can file a compliance complaint with CDE.","offer_to_draft":"I can draft a formal letter to your school district asserting your right to consent to evaluation and services under California law, and requesting they proceed based on your consent. This can help make your position clear and create a paper trail.","draft_type":"iep_email","sources":["CA Ed Code §56506.5 (definition of 'parent' for special education consent)","CA Ed Code §56321 (evaluation request and timelines)"]}

question: I think my child's therapist through RC isn't very good but I'm afraid if I complain they'll take away the service entirely
expectedBehavior: Fear-based question. Must reassure that requesting a different provider does NOT risk losing the service. Explain how to request a vendor change through the IPP. Warm and encouraging.
response: {"success":true,"category":"regional-center","urgency":"medium","emotional_state":"stressed","needs_action":true,"tone_level":"collaborative","empathy":"That fear is completely understandable — when you finally get services, the last thing you want is to lose them by speaking up. But you're not stuck with an ineffective provider, and raising quality concerns is actually part of the process.","clarifying_question":null,"answer":"Regional Center of the East Bay funds the therapy, but they can't punish you for wanting better quality care. The key is to frame this as 'this provider isn't a good fit for Teddy' rather than 'I'm filing a complaint.' You can request a different provider or agency without drama — it happens all the time. Your service coordinator should help you find alternatives, and the funding stays in place while you transition.","action_steps":[{"step":1,"action":"Email your service coordinator at RCEB","who":"Your assigned SC (check your last IPP paperwork for their email)","timeline":"This week","script":"Hi [SC name], I wanted to check in about Teddy's therapy with [provider name]. We've been working together for [timeframe], but I'm not seeing the progress we'd hoped for. I'd like to explore other provider options that might be a better fit for his needs. Can we schedule a time to discuss alternatives? I want to make sure there's no gap in his services during any transition. Thank you!"},{"step":2,"action":"If your SC is slow to respond or unhelpful, request an IPP meeting","who":"Same SC, or their supervisor if needed","timeline":"If no response within 1 week","script":"I'd like to request an IPP meeting to discuss changing Teddy's therapy provider. RCEB needs to schedule this within 30 days — please send me available dates. I want to review provider options and ensure continuity of care."},{"step":3,"action":"Document what's not working","who":"Keep notes for yourself","timeline":"Before your SC conversation or IPP meeting","script":"Write down specific examples: lack of progress on goals, therapist cancellations, poor communication, techniques that aren't evidence-based, etc. You don't need to attack anyone — just have clear reasons why this isn't the right fit."}],"rights_reminder":"Your IPP can be changed anytime you request it — Regional Center must hold the meeting within 30 days. You're an equal member of the IPP team, and you have the right to choose providers that meet your child's needs.","watch_out":"Stay collaborative in tone — 'not a good fit' is much easier for RC to work with than 'this therapist is terrible.' Focus on what Teddy needs rather than what the provider is doing wrong. Also, make sure you have specific examples ready — vague concerns are easier to dismiss.","offer_to_draft":"I can draft that initial email to your service coordinator requesting a provider change conversation — it'll strike the right balance between being clear about your concerns and staying collaborative.","draft_type":"rc_request","sources":[]}
